name: Siftables
short_description: A platform of compact, sensing, display-equipped tiles that people manipulate as a physical group to interact with digital information.
long_description: Siftables, introduced by David Merrill, Jeevan Kalanithi, and Pattie Maes at TEI 2007, are small cookie-sized devices that each carry a graphical display, motion sensing (tilt, shake, rotation via an accelerometer), neighbor detection, and wireless communication. By physically arranging, tilting, and clustering the tiles, users manipulate collections of digital media and content directly with their hands, much like rearranging physical blocks. As an early embodiment of tangible and sensor-network user interfaces, it matters to physical AI and interaction design because it shows how distributed, manipulable objects can make abstract digital information graspable and spatially legible without screens, keyboards, or mice.
ai_note: Siftables is a tangible sensor-network interface; behavior comes from accelerometer/neighbor sensing and rule-based application logic, with no machine learning running in the system.

images.caption: Siftables tiles being manipulated as a group, from the TEI 2007 paper figures.
images.alt_text: A cluster of small square electronic tiles, each with a graphical display, arranged together on a surface and handled by a person.

sources.title: Siftables: towards sensor network user interfaces
sources.publisher: ACM (Proceedings of the 1st International Conference on Tangible and Embedded Interaction, TEI '07)

interactions.title: Push tiles into a pile to group them
interactions.description: Shove several photo-bearing tiles together into a cluster and the system reads the near-simultaneous bumps through their accelerometers and infrared neighbor sensors, binding those images into one group on the host computer the way you would sort prints into stacks on a table.
interactions.source_label: Merrill et al., TEI '07 — Photo Sorting

interactions.title: Shake yes or no
interactions.description: Shake a single tile vertically or horizontally to hand the system a positive or negative answer, a gestural stand-in for clicking confirm or cancel without any buttons.
interactions.source_label: Merrill et al., TEI '07 — Yes/No gesture

interactions.title: Snap a tile down to clear it
interactions.description: Give a tile a sharp downward 'sugar-pack snap' and it drops its current data association, wiping the slate so the tile can take on a new piece of content.
interactions.source_label: Merrill et al., TEI '07 — Sugar Pack Snap

interactions.title: Thump the table to reshuffle the deck
interactions.description: Bang your fist on the surface so every tile jolts at once; the shared accelerometer spike is read as a single command to swap a fresh set of data associations onto the whole collection.
interactions.source_label: Merrill et al., TEI '07 — Thump gesture

interactions.title: Gather a group into one tile
interactions.description: With a single gesture on one member of an established pile, that tile is made to stand in for all the data in the group, collapsing a cluster down to a single representative handle you can carry off.
interactions.source_label: Merrill et al., TEI '07 — Gather gesture

interactions.title: Mix paint by setting tiles side by side
interactions.description: In David Merrill's TED demo, placing a tile holding one 'bucket' of color next to another blends the two on screen, so a blue tile beside a yellow tile yields green where they meet.

interactions.title: Build an equation or a word by lining up tiles
interactions.description: At TED, Merrill arranged number tiles next to an operator tile to compute sums and grow a Fibonacci sequence, and lined up letter tiles to spell words, turning arithmetic and spelling into something you assemble with your hands.

interactions.title: Sequence music by arranging sound tiles
interactions.description: Merrill's TED talk also showed tiles each holding a sound, played back as a phrase by ordering them in a row, so composing a short loop becomes a matter of physically rearranging blocks.

interactions.title: Tilt a tile to pour content into its neighbor
interactions.description: Lean one tile toward an adjacent one and treat the tilt as a pour, draining items or a value from the raised tile into the lower one — a gesture the onboard accelerometer plus 1cm infrared neighbor sensing already make possible.

interactions.title: A tabletop spelling game for a child
interactions.description: Scatter letter tiles across a table and let a kid drag the right ones into a row to build a word, with each tile flashing or buzzing as it snaps into a correct neighbor — a direct extension of the neighbor-detection and per-tile display the platform was built around.
